title: Forms & Input Patterns
source: Starter KB compiled from trusted UX and accessibility guidance (NN/g form usability + required fields + placeholders; GOV.UK Design System form components; WCAG 2.2 Understanding: Labels or Instructions; Material text-field guidance).
purpose: Provide reference knowledge for designing and evaluating forms and data-entry experiences: field structure, labeling, input selection, progressive disclosure, mobile-friendly entry, and accessibility fundamentals. Intended to reduce friction, prevent mistakes, and improve completion rates.
scope: Applies to user-facing form and input experiences across web and mobile: field layout, labels, helper text, required/optional signaling, input types, formatting, grouping, multi-step forms, review/confirm steps, and assistive-technology compatibility. Excludes general error-state patterns beyond forms (covered by the Errors KB) and excludes assistant behavior, output formatting rules, schema enforcement, and runtime tool/DS rules.
definitions: A structured interaction where users enter or confirm information to complete a task (e.g., sign up, checkout, profile update).
Persistent text that identifies what data is expected for an input. Labels should be visible in most cases and located close to the field.
Supporting guidance that clarifies what to enter or how the data will be used; should be used only when it reduces confusion or errors.
Optional in-field hint text. Placeholders are not a substitute for labels and may reduce usability/accessibility when used as the only cue.
Revealing complexity only when needed (e.g., show additional fields after a selection) to reduce cognitive load.
Browser/OS capability that pre-fills known user data (e.g., name, address). Good form design supports autofill and avoids breaking it.
rulesConstraints: Every input must have a clear label or instructions identifying what data is expected; ensure each option in radios/checkboxes has an appropriate label.
Prefer visible, persistent labels; do not rely on placeholder-only labels.
Place labels close to the fields they describe; avoid ambiguous spacing where a label could map to multiple fields.
Group related fields visually and conceptually (sections, headings, spacing) to reduce cognitive load.
Signal required vs optional clearly and consistently (e.g., mark required fields; do not force users to infer).
Choose the simplest input that satisfies the requirement (e.g., short list -> radio; long list -> select/autocomplete; free text only when needed).
Minimize typing: use defaults, smart suggestions, autofill, and constrained inputs where appropriate.
Mobile entry: use appropriate input modes/keyboards (email, numeric) and avoid patterns that cause invalid entry (e.g., numeric spinners for non-quantities).
Avoid unnecessary validation complexity (overly strict formats) when you can accept flexible input and normalize it.
For sensitive fields (passwords, payment), prioritize clarity, trust signals, and compatibility with password managers; avoid designs that block paste or autofill without a strong reason.

do: Use clear, specific labels written in plain language and aligned consistently (often above inputs for mobile and shorter forms).
Group fields into meaningful sections with short headings (e.g., "Billing address", "Payment details").
Ask only what you need; remove non-essential fields or make them optional.
Use helper text when users commonly make mistakes or need context (format examples, what the data is used for).
Prefer selection controls over free text when the set of valid answers is known and small.
Support autofill: keep predictable field names and avoid splitting fields in ways that break autofill (unless necessary).
Support copy/paste and password managers for credentials; allow show/hide password.
Use input constraints that match real-world behavior (accept spaces/dashes in phone numbers; accept uppercase/lowercase emails; normalize where feasible).
Make the primary action clear ("Continue", "Save changes") and match it to a clear step boundary.
For multi-step flows, show progress and allow back navigation without losing entered data.
dont: Don't use placeholder text as the only label or rely on disappearing hints.
Don't scatter labels far from inputs or use ambiguous alignment.
Don't ask for information you don't need at that point in the flow.
Don't force users into repeated submit-fail cycles; design to prevent mistakes up front (constraints, examples, defaults).
Don't use dropdowns for short, common choices (radios are faster and more visible).
Don't require users to remember information from previous steps if you can show it or summarize it.
Don't break autofill/password managers with unusual field structures or blocking paste without a strong justification.
Don't use numeric steppers/spinners for things that are identifiers (phone, ZIP, credit card).

examples: Label: "Phone number"  Hint: "Include country code if outside the US (for example, +44 20 1234 5678)."
Use a consistent required indicator and explain it once (e.g., "* Required"). Mark required fields rather than only optional fields.
For 2–6 mutually exclusive choices: use radio buttons (visible options). For a long list: use autocomplete with search.
Use separate fields for address line 1/2, city, state/region, postal code; support autofill; allow optional address line 2.
Label: "Password"  Helper: "At least 12 characters." Provide show/hide toggle and allow paste; avoid overly complex rules unless required.
edgeCases: When the user is entering identifiers (account numbers, phone, card), avoid controls that imply arithmetic (steppers) and allow common formatting characters (spaces/dashes) where possible.
When a form is long, prefer multi-step with clear grouping and a review step; preserve data across steps and allow back without loss.
When information is conditional (e.g., shipping different from billing), use progressive disclosure with clear toggles and preserve hidden-field values appropriately.
When the design uses custom components (custom select/date picker), define keyboard interaction and focus behavior explicitly and ensure accessible labeling.
When localization is expected, keep labels short, avoid idioms, and ensure layout can expand for longer translated strings.
When collecting sensitive data, add trust cues and transparent explanations (why needed, how used), and avoid dark-pattern phrasing or unnecessary collection.
